title: Children and Families Act 2014
jurisdiction: England and Wales (with some UK-wide employment provisions)
chapters: 139 sections + 7 Schedules in 10 Parts. A broad omnibus Act covering children's social care, SEN, adoption, family justice, and employment. The EMPLOYMENT-RELEVANT provisions are concentrated in Parts 7-9: Part 7 (ss.117-123) — SHARED PARENTAL LEAVE AND PAY, Part 8 (ss.124-131) — TIME OFF FOR ANTENATAL APPOINTMENTS AND ADOPTION MEETINGS, Part 9 (ss.131A-132) — RIGHT TO REQUEST FLEXIBLE WORKING EXTENDED TO ALL EMPLOYEES. These provisions insert new sections into the ERA 1996 and amend the Social Security Contributions and Benefits Act 1992. Implemented by the Shared Parental Leave Regulations 2014 (SI 2014/3050) and the Statutory Shared Parental Pay (General) Regulations 2014 (SI 2014/3051).

parliamentaryOrigin.introducedBy: Government Bill — Conservative-Liberal Democrat Coalition Government, presented by the Secretary of State for Education
parliamentaryOrigin.commencementNotes: Employment provisions phased: flexible working extended to all employees from 30 June 2014. Time off for antenatal appointments from 1 October 2014. Shared parental leave and pay provisions from 1 December 2014, applying to children due or placed for adoption on or after 5 April 2015. Additional paternity leave was ABOLISHED from 5 April 2015 — replaced by shared parental leave.

significantAmendments.effect: From 6 April 2024, the right to request flexible working became a DAY-ONE right (no 26-week qualifying period). Employees can make 2 requests per 12-month period (was 1). Employer must respond within 2 months (was 3). Employer must consult the employee before refusing. These amendments build on the foundation laid by the CFA 2014.

significantAmendments.instrument: Protection from Redundancy (Pregnancy and Family Leave) Act 2023
significantAmendments.effect: Extended redundancy protection for employees on shared parental leave — see redundancyprotection2023.json.

precedingDocuments.title: Additional Paternity Leave Regulations 2010 (ABOLISHED)
precedingDocuments.significance: The predecessor system that allowed fathers to take up to 26 weeks of additional paternity leave if the mother returned to work early. SPL replaced this with a more flexible system that allows BOTH parents to share 50 weeks of leave in any pattern they choose.

leadingCases.name: Ali v Capita Customer Management Ltd / Hextall v Chief Constable of Leicestershire Police
leadingCases.principle: An employer's enhanced maternity pay policy that paid mothers full salary during maternity leave but only statutory pay during shared parental leave did NOT constitute sex discrimination against fathers. The differential treatment was justified because maternity leave serves a different purpose (health and wellbeing of the mother after childbirth) from shared parental leave (shared childcare).
leadingCases.heldText: The purposes of maternity leave (protecting the health of the mother) and shared parental leave (facilitating shared childcare) are fundamentally different. Different pay treatment for different types of leave is not direct sex discrimination.
leadingCases.significance: Critical for employers who offer enhanced maternity pay but only statutory shared parental pay. The Court of Appeal confirmed this is lawful — but employers should be aware that the political and cultural landscape may lead to future legislative change. Many employers now choose to equalise pay as a matter of policy even though the law does not require it.

parts.title: Part 1 — Shared Parental Leave: The Framework

sections.num: CFA 2014 Part 7 (ss.117-123) + Shared Parental Leave Regulations 2014
sections.title: How shared parental leave works — eligibility, entitlement, notice, and employer obligations
sections.summary: Shared parental leave (SPL) allows eligible parents to share up to 50 weeks of leave and 37 weeks of statutory pay in the first year after the birth or adoption of a child. The mother (or primary adopter) must first curtail her maternity (or adoption) leave — the remaining balance then becomes available as SPL for either or both parents. SPL can be taken in continuous blocks or, with the employer's agreement, in discontinuous blocks. Both parents can be on leave simultaneously. The system is deliberately flexible but procedurally complex — the notice requirements involve multiple documents and strict timescales.
sections.text:
ELIGIBILITY:
To qualify for SPL, the employee must:
(a) Have at least 26 weeks' continuous employment with their employer by the end of the 15th week before the EWC (expected week of childbirth) or by the matching date for adoption.
(b) Still be employed by the same employer at the start of each period of SPL.
(c) Share the main responsibility for the child with the other parent.
(d) The OTHER parent must meet the 'employment and earnings test': worked for at least 26 out of the 66 weeks before the EWC/matching date AND earned at least £30 per week (gross) in at least 13 of those weeks. The other parent does NOT need to be an employee — self-employment qualifies.

ENTITLEMENT:
(a) Maximum leave: 50 weeks (52 weeks maternity leave minus the 2 weeks compulsory maternity leave).
(b) Maximum paid leave: 37 weeks at the statutory rate (39 weeks maternity pay minus the 2 compulsory weeks).
(c) Statutory Shared Parental Pay (ShPP): the lower of £184.03 per week (2025-26 rate) or 90% of average weekly earnings. NOTE: unlike SMP, there is no 6-week period at 90% of earnings for ShPP — it is the flat rate throughout.
(d) Leave must be taken before the child's first birthday (or first anniversary of placement).
(e) Leave is taken in COMPLETE WEEKS — minimum period is one week.

THE CURTAILMENT REQUIREMENT:
(a) The mother must CURTAIL her maternity leave to make the balance available as SPL.
(b) She must give a curtailment notice specifying the date her maternity leave will end.
(c) Once given, the curtailment notice is BINDING — except in limited circumstances: the notice was given before birth and is withdrawn within 6 weeks of birth; the other parent dies; neither parent qualifies for SPL.
(d) Maternity leave CANNOT be restarted once curtailed.

NOTICE REQUIREMENTS (8 WEEKS):
(a) NOTICE OF ENTITLEMENT AND INTENTION: at least 8 weeks before the first period of SPL. Must include: dates and amounts of leave and pay each parent intends to take, a declaration that the eligibility conditions are met, the other parent's details and consent.
(b) PERIOD OF LEAVE NOTICE ('booking notice'): at least 8 weeks before each period of SPL starts. Specifies the start and end dates.
(c) The employee can give up to 3 booking notices (i.e., request up to 3 separate blocks of SPL) — unless the employer agrees to more.
(d) CONTINUOUS leave: the employer MUST accept a request for a single continuous block.
(e) DISCONTINUOUS leave: the employer can REFUSE a request for non-consecutive blocks. If refused, the employee can: take the total leave as a single continuous block starting on the proposed start date, withdraw the notice, or agree an alternative pattern.
(f) VARIATION NOTICE: at least 8 weeks before any variation to agreed leave.

SPLIT DAYS (Shared Parental Leave In Touch days):
(a) Up to 20 SPLIT days per parent (in addition to the 10 KIT days available during maternity leave).
(b) The employee can work up to 20 days during SPL without bringing the leave to an end.
(c) Both parties must agree — neither can compel the other.

EMPLOYMENT RIGHTS DURING SPL:
(a) The employee retains all contractual benefits during SPL (except remuneration — unless the employer offers enhanced SPL pay).
(b) Pension contributions: the employer must continue to contribute based on the employee's normal pay (not the statutory rate) during paid SPL.
(c) Right to return to the same job after SPL of 26 weeks or less (in isolation or combined with maternity leave). After more than 26 weeks: right to return to the same job or a suitable alternative.
(d) Protection from detriment and automatic unfair dismissal for taking or proposing to take SPL.
sections.keyPrinciple: SPL is the most procedurally complex family leave entitlement in UK employment law. The notice requirements are layered, the eligibility conditions involve BOTH parents, and the interaction between maternity leave curtailment and SPL entitlement requires careful calculation. The HR manager must understand: (a) who is eligible, (b) how leave is divided between the parents, (c) the curtailment requirement and its irrevocability, (d) the 8-week notice periods, (e) the distinction between continuous leave (must accept) and discontinuous leave (can refuse), and (f) the employment rights that continue during SPL.
refs: ERA 1996 ss.75E-75K (shared parental leave — inserted by CFA 2014)
Ali v Capita [2019] EWCA Civ 900 (enhanced maternity pay vs SPL pay — not discrimination)

regulatoryGuidance.title: Shared Parental Leave and Pay: Employers' Technical Guide
regulatoryGuidance.relevance: Comprehensive Government guidance for employers on SPL eligibility, notice requirements, pay calculations, and practical management.

commonErrors: Not understanding the curtailment requirement — SPL is only available once the mother CURTAILS her maternity leave. Without a curtailment notice, SPL cannot begin. The curtailment is binding and maternity leave cannot be restarted

Confusing maternity pay entitlements with ShPP — SMP includes 6 weeks at 90% of earnings followed by 33 weeks at the flat rate. ShPP is the flat rate ONLY throughout. Enhanced maternity pay policies do NOT automatically extend to SPL (confirmed by Ali v Capita)
Refusing a continuous block of SPL — if the employee requests a SINGLE CONTINUOUS block, the employer MUST accept it. The employer can only refuse DISCONTINUOUS leave requests

Not knowing that both parents can be on SPL simultaneously — SPL allows BOTH parents to take leave at the same time, provided the combined total does not exceed the available balance. This is a deliberate feature, not an error
Failing to maintain pension contributions during paid SPL — the employer must continue pension contributions based on the employee's NORMAL pay, not the statutory ShPP rate
Not having an SPL policy — the procedural complexity of SPL makes a written policy essential. Without a policy, neither the employer nor the employee can navigate the notice requirements effectively

Treating SPL as a niche entitlement that nobody uses — take-up has been low (approximately 2-4% of eligible parents) but is increasing. The HR manager should be prepared for SPL requests and should not treat them as unusual or inconvenient

parts.title: Part 2 — Time Off for Antenatal Appointments and Flexible Working

sections.title: Time off for fathers/partners, adoption meetings, and the flexible working framework
sections.summary: Part 8 of the CFA 2014 introduced two new rights: (a) the right for fathers and partners to take UNPAID time off to attend up to 2 antenatal appointments (maximum 6.5 hours per appointment), and (b) the right for adopters and their partners to take time off to attend adoption meetings. Part 9 extended the right to request flexible working from parents/carers only to ALL employees with 26 weeks' service (subsequently amended by the Employment Relations (Flexible Working) Act 2023 to make it a Day-One right from 6 April 2024).
sections.text:
TIME OFF FOR ANTENATAL APPOINTMENTS (Part 8):

(a) MOTHERS: existing right to PAID time off for antenatal appointments (Pregnancy — ERA 1996 s.55). No limit on number of appointments. Unchanged by CFA 2014.

(b) FATHERS/PARTNERS (NEW — CFA 2014 s.127, inserting ERA 1996 s.57ZE):
— Right to UNPAID time off to attend up to 2 antenatal appointments.
— Maximum 6.5 hours per appointment.
— A 'qualifying relationship' means: spouse, civil partner, partner living in an enduring family relationship, or the father of the child. Includes same-sex partners and intended parents in surrogacy arrangements.
— Day-one right — no qualifying period.
— The employer cannot require evidence of the appointment (unlike the mother's right, where evidence can be requested after the first appointment).
— Detriment and automatic unfair dismissal protection for exercising the right.

(c) ADOPTERS (NEW — CFA 2014 s.128, inserting ERA 1996 s.57ZJ):
— Primary adopter: PAID time off for up to 5 adoption meetings.
— Secondary adopter (partner): UNPAID time off for up to 2 adoption meetings.

FLEXIBLE WORKING (Part 9):

(a) CFA 2014 extended the right to request flexible working from parents/carers to ALL EMPLOYEES with 26 weeks' continuous service (from 30 June 2014).

(b) The Employment Relations (Flexible Working) Act 2023 further amended the framework from 6 April 2024:
— RIGHT FROM DAY ONE — no 26-week qualifying period.
— 2 requests per 12-month period (was 1).
— Employer must respond within 2 MONTHS (was 3).
— Employer must CONSULT the employee before refusing.
— The employer can still refuse on one of 8 statutory grounds (ERA 1996 s.80G(1)(b)): burden of additional costs, detrimental effect on ability to meet customer demand, inability to reorganise work among existing staff, inability to recruit additional staff, detrimental impact on quality, detrimental impact on performance, insufficiency of work during proposed working periods, planned structural changes.

(c) A flexible working request is a request for a PERMANENT change to terms and conditions — not a temporary arrangement. The employer's response sets the employee's ongoing contractual terms.

(d) The ACAS Code of Practice on Flexible Working Requests (2024) has statutory force — failure to follow it may lead to an increased award (up to 25%) in tribunal proceedings.
sections.keyPrinciple: The CFA 2014 established the modern family-friendly employment framework: SPL for shared childcare, time off for fathers/partners at antenatal appointments, and flexible working for all employees. The 2023/2024 amendments have strengthened all three pillars. The HR manager must understand these as interconnected rights that collectively support working parents — not as isolated obligations.
refs: ERA 1996 s.57ZE (time off for antenatal appointments — fathers/partners)
ERA 1996 s.57ZJ (time off for adoption meetings)
ERA 1996 ss.80F-80I (flexible working — as amended by ER(FW)A 2023)
ACAS Code of Practice on Flexible Working Requests (2024)
commonErrors: Not knowing that fathers/partners have a right to time off for antenatal appointments — this is a Day-One right, unpaid, for up to 2 appointments. The employer cannot refuse

Requiring evidence of the appointment from the father/partner — unlike the mother's right (where evidence can be requested after the first appointment), the father/partner's right does NOT allow the employer to require evidence

Not updating the flexible working policy for the April 2024 changes — flexible working is now a Day-One right, employees can make 2 requests per year, the employer must respond within 2 months, and the employer must consult before refusing

Treating a flexible working request as a request for a temporary arrangement — it is a request for a PERMANENT change to terms and conditions. The employer should be clear about this in their response
Not following the ACAS Code of Practice on Flexible Working Requests — the Code has statutory force. Failure to follow it can lead to an increased award in tribunal proceedings

pendingAmendments.status: The ERA 2025 may introduce further reforms to shared parental leave, potentially including extended paid leave and simplified notice requirements. Monitor for secondary legislation.
pendingAmendments.aceiImpact: MODERATE — SPL reform would affect all employers

pendingAmendments.status: SPL take-up remains low (2-4% of eligible parents). The Government has committed to reviewing the system. Monitor for reform proposals.
